title: Origami masu box
subtitle: Folding a traditional Japanese square box with a lid
excerpt: The masu box is a traditional Japanese square measuring box adapted as an origami model. Folded from two sheets of equal-sized paper, it produces a lidded box that sits flat, holds its shape without glue, and can be made from a single 15 cm sheet to hold small objects. This tutorial folds both box and lid from the same sheet size.
sourceNotes: John Montroll, Origami Inside-Out (1993, Dover Publications), box chapter. The masu box fold is a well-documented traditional Japanese construction; this version follows Montroll's sequence for the modular box-and-lid pair.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold where the paper bends toward you, forming a valley crease on the visible face. Indicated on origami diagrams by a dashed line.
A fold in which part of the paper is folded in the opposite direction by opening the layers and changing the direction of a crease from valley to mountain.

content.text: The masu box folds from one square of paper; the lid requires a second square cut 2-3 mm larger to fit over the box. Using kami paper (coloured one side, white the other), the box interior is white and the exterior shows the colour, which is the traditional presentation. Larger sheets produce deeper boxes: a 20 cm sheet makes a box of about 6.5 cm square and 3 cm deep.
Fold the diagonal and central creases

Place the coloured side face-down. Fold corner to corner in both diagonals; unfold. Fold edge to edge in both directions; unfold. You now have a grid of eight crease lines meeting at the centre. All the following folds build from this grid.
Fold the four corners to the centre
Fold each of the four corners to the exact centre point using a
. The result is a smaller square. Press each fold firmly with the bone folder.
Fold two opposite edges to the centre

Fold the top edge down to the centre crease and the bottom edge up to the centre crease. Unfold. You now have additional crease lines parallel to the top and bottom. Repeat this step for the left and right edges, then unfold those too. These crease lines mark the box sides.
Open two sides and raise the box

Open out the left and right flaps (unfold the corners that were tucked in). Fold the top and bottom edges up to the centre, keeping the sides folded at 90°. The opened left and right corner flaps will now fold up and over the top to form the two remaining box walls. Tuck the triangular corner points down inside the box. Square the corners with the bone folder. Repeat the full process with a slightly larger sheet for the lid.

The masu box made from 220 gsm cartridge or origami washi holds its shape better than kami for practical use: it works as a desk tidy, gift box, or seed packet container. A watercolour wash applied to a cartridge sheet before folding gives a soft tonal variation to the finished box that printed paper cannot replicate.
